components.label: Producer / Consumer Applications
components.description: Producers write events to topic partitions; Consumer groups read from partitions at their own committed offset; both use the Kafka client library for broker discovery and offset management

components.label: Kafka Broker Cluster
components.description: Each broker stores partition logs for which it is the leader; handles producer writes, consumer reads, and replication to follower brokers; horizontally scalable by adding brokers

components.label: Append-Only Partition Log (Disk)
components.description: Each partition is a sequence of segment files on disk; new messages are appended to the active segment; sequential disk I/O enables 1M+ writes/sec per broker; messages are never modified or deleted until retention expires

components.label: Topic Partition Layer
components.description: Topics are divided into N partitions; partition assignment determines which broker is the leader; producers hash message keys to partitions for ordering guarantees within a key

components.label: KRaft Controller (Cluster Metadata)
components.description: KRaft (or ZooKeeper in legacy setups) manages broker metadata, partition leadership, ISR lists, and topic configuration; it is the control plane — it never stores message data

connections.label: Producer: write event to partition leader
Append event to partition log segment on disk
ACK to producer after ISR replication (acks=all)
Consumer: fetch from partition at committed offset
Controller assigns partition leadership, manages ISR

keyInsights: The append-only log is the fundamental abstraction: events are never modified or removed until retention expires; a consumer can read the same event 100 times, multiple consumer groups can read the same log simultaneously, and any consumer can replay from offset 0 — none of this is possible with a traditional queue that deletes on ack

Partition-level ordering guarantee: Kafka guarantees order within a partition, not across partitions; to maintain order for a specific entity (e.g. all events for user_id=42), hash the entity key to a partition — all events for that key always land in the same partition and are consumed in order

Consumer offset ownership decouples consumers: each consumer group independently tracks its read position in __consumer_offsets; a slow batch analytics consumer reading at 1K events/sec does not affect a fast stream processing consumer reading at 1M events/sec from the same topic

ISR (in-sync replicas) is the durability contract: with acks=all, a producer write is only acknowledged after all ISR members have written it to their local log; if a follower falls behind (outside the replica.lag.time.max config), it is removed from ISR — the ack contract applies only to the current ISR set

KRaft vs ZooKeeper: ZooKeeper was the legacy metadata store but introduced operational complexity (separate cluster to manage); KRaft embeds the Raft consensus algorithm directly into Kafka brokers, eliminating ZooKeeper entirely in Kafka 3.x — same guarantees, simpler operations

tradeoffs.decision: Append-only log over traditional message queue
tradeoffs.pro: Enables replay, multiple independent consumer groups, time-travel debugging, and event sourcing — none possible with delete-on-consume queues
tradeoffs.con: Storage grows until retention expires; requires consumers to manage their own offsets; more complex client library than a simple queue

tradeoffs.pro: Global ordering would require a single partition — no parallelism; partition-level ordering enables N-way parallel consumption while still guaranteeing order for each entity
tradeoffs.con: Cross-partition ordering requires application-level sequencing; consumers must handle out-of-order messages across partitions if they care about global sequence

tradeoffs.pro: Zero data loss on broker failure — message is on at least replication-factor copies before ack
tradeoffs.con: Higher produce latency (must wait for all ISR members); if ISR shrinks to 1 broker, durability guarantee weakens

antiPatterns: Using Kafka as a task queue with delete-on-consume semantics — Kafka does not support this natively; messages expire by time/size, not by acknowledgement; use RabbitMQ or SQS for true delete-on-consume task queues

Creating one partition per consumer to avoid rebalancing — partition count is the parallelism ceiling; over-partitioning wastes resources and increases rebalance time; partition count should match the peak consumer parallelism needed

Committing offsets before processing is complete — if the consumer crashes after commit but before processing, those events are silently skipped; commit offsets only after successful processing (at-least-once), or use a transactional producer for exactly-once semantics

Storing large payloads (>1MB) directly in Kafka messages — Kafka is optimised for small, frequent events; large payloads cause network saturation and broker memory pressure; store large objects in S3 and put only the object reference in the Kafka message
